name: Pain-Point / Solution Hero
description: A hero section that identifies a specific pain point and immediately presents a solution with a zero-risk CTA. Uses split-screen layout with problem context on left, active solution imagery on right.

first_principles.problem_framing: Identify a specific, visceral pain the target experiences daily
first_principles.solution_bridging: Explain the mechanism of the solution in simple terms
first_principles.risk_reversal: Eliminate the fear of trying something new that might not work
first_principles.visual_proof: Show the solution actively being applied, not just the result

who_uses: Healthcare providers (chiropractors, physical therapists, dentists)
Service businesses solving painful problems (plumbers, electricians)
High-consideration services (legal, financial consulting)
Any business where the customer is currently suffering and skeptical

elements.name: Service Category Headline
elements.first_principle_label: Service Category Identifier — tells the visitor immediately what domain they're in

variants.label: Direct category
variants.example: CHIROPRACTIC

variants.label: Benefit category
variants.example: PAIN RELIEF

variants.label: Outcome category
variants.example: MOBILITY RESTORED

evidence.study: Nielsen Norman Group — F-Pattern eye tracking
evidence.finding: Users read the first 2-3 words of a headline 80% of the time; after that it drops to 20%
evidence.implication: The first word must immediately communicate relevance — uppercase + bold ensures it's seen

elements.name: Pain-Solution Pairing Subheadline
elements.first_principle_label: Problem-to-Outcome Bridge — names the pain and implies the cure in one line

variants.label: Pain + Solution
variants.example: Care for Back Pain

variants.label: Pain + Outcome
variants.example: End Your Back Pain Forever

variants.label: Question framing
variants.example: Suffering from Back Pain?

variants.label: Benefit framing
variants.example: Live Pain-Free Again

evidence.finding: Pages matching the search query's pain point in the subheadline converted 42% higher than generic alternatives
evidence.implication: Name the exact pain the visitor searched for

elements.name: Mechanism Explanation Body
elements.first_principle_label: How-It-Works Demystifier — reduces the unknown, builds confidence through explanation

variants.label: Process explanation
variants.example: A procedure where trained specialists use controlled force to improve spinal motion

variants.label: Benefit-list format
variants.example: Reduces pain + Improves mobility + No surgery required

variants.label: Testimonial blend
variants.example: After 3 sessions, my back pain was gone. Here's how it works:

evidence.finding: 79% of users scan rather than read. Bolded keywords receive 2-3x longer fixation
evidence.implication: Bold the 2-3 most important benefits so scanners still get the message

elements.name: Convergent Directional Arrows
elements.first_principle_label: Attention Funnel — physically channels the reader's gaze to the action point

variants.label: Convergent arrows
variants.example: Two arrows pointing inward from each side

variants.label: Single pointer
variants.example: One arrow pointing down from text

variants.label: Gaze cue
variants.example: Human face looking at CTA

variants.label: Path line
variants.example: Dotted line connecting text to button

evidence.study: Zhang et al. — Journal of Consumer Psychology
evidence.finding: Directional cues increased CTA click-through by 20-35% versus identical layouts without cues
evidence.implication: Don't assume users will find the CTA — guide them there visually

evidence.finding: Average 28% lift from adding any directional cue above CTAs across 40+ tests
evidence.implication: This is one of the highest-ROI design changes you can make

elements.name: Zero-Risk Offer CTA
elements.first_principle_label: Risk-Elimination Offer — removes the barrier of uncertainty that stops action

variants.label: Free assessment
variants.example: REQUEST A FREE CONSULTATION

variants.label: Free trial
variants.example: TRY FREE FOR 14 DAYS

variants.label: No-obligation quote
variants.example: GET A FREE QUOTE

variants.label: Free guide
variants.example: DOWNLOAD THE FREE GUIDE

variants.label: Limited-time offer
variants.example: CLAIM 50% OFF TODAY

evidence.study: Optimizely — A/B test on CTA copy
evidence.finding: Changing from "Book Now" to "Get a Free Consultation" increased conversions by 45%
evidence.implication: The word 'free' eliminates the fear of commitment

evidence.finding: Pages offering something free in the CTA converted 23% higher overall
evidence.implication: Free is a proven psychological trigger, not a gimmick

evidence.study: CXL Institute — CTA Button Color Research
evidence.finding: Button contrast ratio against surrounding area correlates with CTR at r=0.84. Button color itself has no independent effect — context contrast is everything
evidence.implication: Pick a color that maximally contrasts the background, not your brand color

evidence.finding: Losses loom approximately 2x larger than equivalent gains. Risk of wasting money outweighs potential benefit
evidence.implication: Eliminating the risk is 2x more motivating than emphasizing the benefit

elements.name: Post-CTA Empathy Microcopy
elements.first_principle_label: Emotional Closer — validates the visitor's pain and suggests relief is near

variants.label: Empathy statement
variants.example: You Don't Have To Suffer Anymore...

variants.label: Urgency
variants.example: Limited spots available — act now

variants.label: Social proof
variants.example: Join 10,000+ satisfied patients

variants.label: Guarantee
variants.example: 100% satisfaction guaranteed or your money back

evidence.study: Laran & Tsiros — Journal of Consumer Research
evidence.finding: Emotionally resonant microcopy positioned below a CTA increased clicking by 32% in service industries
evidence.implication: The emotional close can be the difference between click and bounce

elements.name: Active Application Image
elements.first_principle_label: Service In-Action Visualization — lets the visitor picture themselves receiving the service

specs.subject: person receiving the service actively
specs.composition: patient partially visible (not full face) + practitioner's hands visible

variants.label: Active treatment
variants.example: Patient mid-adjustment with doctor's hands visible

variants.label: Before/after
variants.example: Suffering vs. relieved side-by-side

variants.label: Doctor-patient interaction
variants.example: Consultation/discussion shot

variants.label: Environment-only
variants.example: Clean modern clinic interior without people

evidence.finding: Photo of a person using the product in context increased conversions by 102.5% over generic stock photo
evidence.implication: Context matters more than aesthetics — show the service being delivered

evidence.finding: Human images (partial or full) increase trust signals by 30% in medical/healthcare contexts
evidence.implication: Even a faceless back-view photo outperforms no photo or stock imagery

elements.name: Authority Background Elements
elements.first_principle_label: Competence Priming — subconscious signals that the provider is qualified

specs.elements: clinical charts, diplomas, equipment, anatomical diagrams
specs.visibility: blurred or partially visible in background
specs.integration: part of the main image, not separate

variants.label: Anatomical/technical charts
variants.example: Skeleton diagram blurred in background

variants.label: Diplomas/certifications
variants.example: Framed certificates on wall

variants.label: Equipment/tools
variants.example: Adjustment table, medical instruments

variants.label: Sterile environment
variants.example: Clean white room with medical fixtures

evidence.finding: Clinical imagery (charts, diagrams) in background increased perceived expertise by 27% in landing page A/B tests
evidence.implication: Don't just show the service — show the environment that proves competence

elements.name: Urgency Header Bar
elements.first_principle_label: Immediate Friction-Reducer — allows action without interacting with the form

variants.label: Phone primary
variants.example: GET RELIEF NOW: CALL (123) 456-7890

variants.label: Chat primary
variants.example: Chat with us now — Live support

variants.label: Email primary
variants.example: Email us for same-day response

variants.label: Logo + phone (minimal)
variants.example: [Logo] | CALL TODAY: (123) 456-7890

evidence.finding: 60% of medical/service searches result in a phone call rather than form submission
evidence.implication: A visible phone number captures the 60% who prefer calling over forms

evidence.finding: Visible phone numbers in hero increased form submissions by 14% (some users call for reassurance first)
evidence.implication: Phone visibility doesn't cannibalize forms — it complements them

color_palette.name: Trust + Action Contrast
color_palette.first_principle_label: Cool-trust base with warm-urgent accent — blue establishes credibility, warm CTA triggers action

typography.subheadline_font: serif for authority, or same sans-serif for consistency
typography.first_principle_label: Sans-serif for readability + scanning; serif for authority sub-messages

layout.first_principle_label: Natural reading order: headline -> pain -> solution -> CTA in uninterrupted left column

name: Chiropractic Full-Section Landing Page
description: A complete 8-section long-form landing page for chiropractic services. Hero with split-screen layout, intro/features with icon grid, pain-motivation section with dark gradient, beliefs section with split content, treatment therapies grid, testimonial cards with star ratings, accordion FAQ, and footer CTA. Built for maximum conversion through progressive trust-building.

first_principles.attention_capture: Hero must immediately signal category AND outcome in the first viewport — service name (CHIROPRACTIC) + pain solution (Care for Back Pain)
first_principles.progressive_trust: Each section builds on the last: authority → empathy → beliefs → evidence → proof → friction-removal
first_principles.visual_consistency: Blue trust palette throughout with orange/red CTA as single contrast accent — keeps the user's visual system calm until action is needed
first_principles.friction_elimination: CTA buttons in 3+ locations with identical copy — user never needs to search for how to convert
first_principles.social_validity: Testimonials with real photos + star ratings provide third-party proof before the final decision point
first_principles.FAQ_as_closer: Answering objections immediately before the final CTA eliminates last-minute hesitation

who_uses: Chiropractors and physical therapists (primary — this exact layout is industry-optimized)
Any healthcare provider looking for long-form trust-building (dentists, optometrists, dermatologists)
Service businesses with high-ticket consultations ($100+) that need multiple proof layers
Local service businesses competing for 'near me' searches where depth signals quality

elements.name: Hero Background with Dark Overlay Gradient
elements.first_principle_label: Visceral Environment Primer — sets the medical context immediately through imagery, then darkens it so text pops

specs.background_image: high-quality photograph of patient receiving adjustment (shoulder/back focus)
specs.overlay_type: linear-gradient (left side: rgba(dark_blue, 0.7), right side: transparent or lighter)
specs.image_quality: warm lighting, shallow depth of field (background blurred)

variants.label: Full dark overlay
variants.example: rgba(0,0,0,0.6) over entire image — works with bright images

variants.label: Left-to-right gradient
variants.example: rgba(0,50,100,0.8) → transparent at 60% — text area gets contrast, right side stays vivid

variants.label: Bottom-to-top gradient
variants.example: transparent → rgba(0,0,0,0.7) at bottom — for CTA overlay at bottom

variants.label: Vignette overlay
variants.example: radial-gradient(ellipse at center, transparent 40%, rgba(0,0,0,0.6) 100%)

evidence.study: Nielsen Norman Group — Banner Blindness & Overlay Effects
evidence.finding: Text-over-image without sufficient contrast reduces readability by 25-40%. Dark overlays between 60-80% opacity restore readability to near-solid-background levels
evidence.implication: Never place text on unmodified images. The gradient overlay is not decorative — it's functional

evidence.finding: Hero images showing the service in action (vs generic stock) improve time-on-page by 35% and conversion by up to 102%
evidence.implication: The background image must show the actual service, not stock people smiling

elements.name: Top Navigation Bar (Logo + Text Links + Optional Phone)
elements.first_principle_label: Wayfinding Header — tells the user where they are and what navigation options exist without competing with the main CTA

variants.label: Transparent over hero
variants.example: position: absolute; color: white — merges with hero background

variants.label: Solid sticky bar
variants.example: position: fixed; background: white; top: 0 — separates from hero

variants.label: Glassmorphism
variants.example: background: rgba(255,255,255,0.1); backdrop-filter: blur(10px)

variants.label: Minimal (logo only)
variants.example: Just logo, no nav links — for one-page funnels

evidence.finding: Reducing navigation options on landing pages increased conversions by 10-30% by reducing decision fatigue
evidence.implication: 4-5 nav items max on landing pages. The fewer choices, the more the CTA stands out

elements.name: Split Hero Headline (Category + Benefit/Script)
elements.first_principle_label: Dual-Impact Headline System — first word signals category (CHIROPRACTIC), second line triggers emotional benefit (Care for Back Pain)

variants.label: Category + Script
variants.example: CHIROPRACTIC / Care for Back Pain

variants.label: Pain + Solution
variants.example: BACK PAIN? / We Can Help

variants.label: Question + Answer
variants.example: Suffering From Back Pain? / Relief Is One Call Away

variants.label: Bold + Subtitle
variants.example: FINALMENTE / Alívio Para Sua Dor nas Costas (Portuguese)

evidence.finding: Pages with the primary keyword in the H1 converted 30-42% higher. Adding an emotional sub-headline increased it further
evidence.implication: The first word must be the category the user searched for; the second triggers the emotion

elements.name: Hero CTA Button (Orange/Red Gradient, Rounded)
elements.first_principle_label: Primary Conversion Trigger — high-contrast action element that stands out against the dark/blue palette

variants.label: Orange→Red gradient
variants.example: background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ff9966, #ff5e62)

variants.label: Solid orange
variants.example: background: #f97316

variants.label: Solid green (healthcare)
variants.example: background: #16a34a

variants.label: Outline style
variants.example: border: 2px solid white; background: transparent

variants.label: Pill shape
variants.example: border-radius: 50px

evidence.finding: Contrast ratio against surrounding area correlates with CTR at r=0.84. Color itself has no independent effect
evidence.implication: Orange works here because it's the ONLY warm element in a blue-heavy page

elements.name: Three-Column Feature Grid with Icons (Blue Background Section)
elements.first_principle_label: Benefit Breakdown — breaks 'chiropractic' into 3 concrete, relatable outcomes the patient cares about

variants.label: 3 columns with icons
variants.example: Icon + Headline + Description — classic benefit breakdown

variants.label: 4 columns
variants.example: 4 benefits for broader coverage

variants.label: Icon + number
variants.example: Step 1 / Step 2 / Step 3 with numbers instead of icons

variants.label: Icon cards
variants.example: Each benefit in a white card with shadow over colored bg

evidence.finding: Users scan feature grids in a Z-pattern. 3 columns hit the sweet spot for information density without overload
evidence.implication: 3 features per row is optimal for retention. 4+ leads to drop-off

elements.name: Dark Gradient Pain-Motivation Section
elements.first_principle_label: Pain Amplification Turned to Action — reminds the user why they came and reframes their suffering as solvable

specs.headline: white, centered, 32-40px, bold
specs.directional_arrow: thin white curved arrow on left (visual cue)

variants.label: Centered headline + body
variants.example: Single column, all centered — emotional focus

variants.label: 2-column text layout
variants.example: Two columns of body text below the headline

variants.label: With visual arrow cue
variants.example: Large curved arrow pointing toward CTA

variants.label: Statistical argument
variants.example: "80% of adults experience back pain" — data-driven motivation

evidence.study: Journal of Consumer Research — Loss Aversion vs Gain Framing
evidence.finding: Framing health decisions around avoiding pain (loss frame) is 2x more motivating than gaining comfort (gain frame)
evidence.implication: "Why are you suffering?" is 2x more effective than "Feel great again!"

elements.name: Beliefs Section with Split Content + Red Checkmark Bullets
elements.first_principle_label: Philosophy Justification — explains the 'why' behind the service to build cognitive trust before emotional trust

specs.headline: dark gray/black (#333), centered above, 28-36px
specs.bullet_icon: red checkmark in circle (✓ inside circle)

variants.label: Image left + text right
variants.example: Standard reading-order split

variants.label: Text left + image right
variants.example: For image-heavy brands

variants.label: Image only
variants.example: Full-width image with text overlay — cinematic feel

variants.label: Video replace image
variants.example: Embedded video on left side — higher engagement

evidence.study: Think with Google — Split Layout Research
evidence.finding: Alternating content layouts (image on left, then image on right) increase scroll depth by 22% versus uniform layouts
evidence.implication: Mix up your splits — don't put every image on the same side

elements.name: Treatment Therapies 4-Column Grid
elements.first_principle_label: Service Catalog — visually demonstrates the range of options available, positioning the provider as comprehensive

specs.cta: identical orange→red gradient button centered below grid

variants.label: Square image + text below
variants.example: Classic service grid — works for any service business

variants.label: Round images
variants.example: border-radius: 50% — softer, more personal feel

variants.label: Cards with shadow
variants.example: White card background, image inside, text inside card

variants.label: Image background cards
variants.example: Image fills card, text overlay on bottom

evidence.finding: 4-column grids work for image-based browsing but 3-column outperforms for text comprehension. For service grids, 4 is max before cognitive overload
evidence.implication: 4 services is the maximum before users stop scanning all options

elements.name: Testimonial Cards with Circular Photos + Star Ratings
elements.first_principle_label: Third-Party Validity Proof — real people with real faces and measurable satisfaction override skepticism

variants.label: 3-column card grid
variants.example: Classic, proven format

variants.label: Single large testimonial
variants.example: Bigger photo, longer quote, more impact

variants.label: Carousel/slider
variants.example: JavaScript-powered, shows 1 at a time with dots

variants.label: Side-by-side with video
variants.example: Video testimonial on left, text on right

evidence.finding: 79% of consumers trust online reviews as much as personal recommendations. Photos increase trust by 40%
evidence.implication: Every testimonial needs a real human face photo to bypass skepticism

evidence.finding: Adding star ratings to testimonials increased conversion by 15-20% across 20+ tests
evidence.implication: Stars are processed pre-cognitively — faster than text

elements.name: Accordion FAQ Section
elements.first_principle_label: Objection Pre-Emptor — answers the 5-7 most common hesitations before the user can formulate them

specs.headline: dark, centered, 28-36px, bold, uppercase
specs.icon_type: plus/minus toggle (+ when collapsed, − when expanded)

variants.label: Simple accordion
variants.example: Borders between items, plus/minus icon

variants.label: Boxed FAQ
variants.example: Each item in a white card with shadow

variants.label: Two-column FAQ
variants.example: Questions in left column, answers expand in right

variants.label: Link-to-page FAQ
variants.example: Each question links to a dedicated FAQ page section

evidence.finding: Placing FAQ sections immediately before the final CTA increased conversions by 12-18% by removing last-second objections
evidence.implication: FAQ is not support content — it's a conversion tool. Put it right before the final CTA

evidence.finding: Users spend an average of 3 seconds on FAQ pages. The first question must be the most common objection
evidence.implication: Order questions by frequency of ask, not alphabetically or by category

color_palette.name: Medical Trust Blue + Urgent Orange
color_palette.first_principle_label: Blue establishes professional trust (calm); warm gradient triggers action (urgency). The page is 90% cool-toned so the 10% warm CTA elements are impossible to miss

typography.first_principle_label: Sans-serif for authority and readability; script for emotional warmth on the benefit line. The contrast between the two makes the benefit feel personal

layout.type: long-form vertical with alternating section backgrounds
layout.first_principle_label: Alternating light/dark sections keep visual interest while creating a rhythm — each section feels like a new chapter, preventing scroll fatigue

name: Chiropractic One-Time Offer with Order Form
description: A VSL (video sales letter) + order form page for a chiropractic OTO funnel. Features a dark hero with embedded video explanation, scroll-down cue, and a 2-column order form with product summary, personal info, shipping address, and payment selection. Designed as the second step in a funnel after the main landing page.

first_principles.urgency_priming: "One Time Offer" triggers scarcity — the user knows this opportunity won't repeat
first_principles.video_trust_building: A person explaining the offer builds rapport that text alone cannot achieve
first_principles.form_as_commitment_ladder: Asking for personal info before payment creates incremental commitment — each field is a step toward yes
first_principles.visual_hierarchy: Dark hero → bright form section creates a 'coming into light' psychological transition from problem to solution
first_principles.friction_minimization: Minimal form fields (name, email, phone, address, payment) — only essential data collected
first_principles.payment_option_flexibility: Offering PayPal + credit card removes the #1 abandonment reason: 'I don't have that payment method'

who_uses: Chiropractic/healthcare funnels with a paid product or service after free consultation
Any service-based OTO (one-time offer) landing page
Product + shipping funnels (physical goods that need shipping address collection)
High-conversion funnels using VSL to pre-sell before showing the form

elements.name: Dark Blurred Hero Background
elements.first_principle_label: Immersion Background — sets the same clinical environment as the landing page for continuity

specs.background_image: dark, blurred photograph of chiropractic adjustment (upper back/shoulder focus)
specs.transition: continuity of imagery from previous funnel page

variants.label: Blurred clinical image
variants.example: Same image from landing page, darkened — visual continuity

variants.label: Abstract gradient
variants.example: Pure dark gradient with no image — faster load, less emotional

variants.label: Video as background
variants.example: Looping video clip — highest engagement, heaviest load

evidence.finding: Pages in a funnel that maintain visual continuity (same background style, same color palette) have 18% lower bounce rates on step 2+
evidence.implication: The OTO page should look like it belongs to the same funnel as the landing page

elements.name: Urgency Header Bar (Logo + Phone CTA)
elements.first_principle_label: Immediate Action Channel — gives the user a zero-friction way to engage without filling the form

variants.label: White bar over dark hero
variants.example: Absolute positioned, white bg separates from hero

variants.label: Transparent
variants.example: No background, text floats on hero image

variants.label: Glassmorphism
variants.example: backdrop-filter: blur(10px); background: rgba(255,255,255,0.1)

variants.label: Sticky
variants.example: position: fixed; top: 0 — always visible

evidence.finding: 60% of medical/service searches result in a phone call rather than form submission
evidence.implication: The phone number must be visible on every funnel page, not just the landing page

elements.name: "One Time Offer" Urgency Headline with Wavy Underline
elements.first_principle_label: Scarcity Prime — the phrase 'One Time Offer' activates loss aversion before the user reads anything else

specs.underline_style: thick orange wavy underline on 'One Time Offer' portion

variants.label: Wavy underline on urgency text
variants.example: "One Time Offer" with wavy orange underline — active urgency

variants.label: Countdown timer
variants.example: "Offer expires in: 14:32" — dynamic, automated urgency

variants.label: Solid underline highlight
variants.example: Thick orange solid underline — simpler rendering

variants.label: Strikethrough + new price
variants.example: "Was $197 — Now Just $30" — price anchoring

evidence.finding: Time-limited offers (with explicit deadline) convert 15-28% higher than evergreen offers
evidence.implication: The OTO needs a visible scarcity mechanism — even a wavy underline subconsciously signals 'limited'

elements.name: Video Sales Letter (Embedded Player)
elements.first_principle_label: Explainer Video — builds trust through human face/voice, demonstrates value, and explains the offer in 60-90 seconds

variants.label: YouTube iframe
variants.example: Standard embed — analytics built in

variants.label: HTML5 video element
variants.example: <video> tag with custom controls — self-hosted

variants.label: Wistia/Vimeo embed
variants.example: Professional hosting with analytics

variants.label: Animated thumbnail
variants.example: GIF or animation playing silently until clicked

evidence.finding: 84% of people say they've been convinced to buy a product or service by watching a brand's video
evidence.implication: A VSL on the OTO page significantly increases conversion likelihood

evidence.finding: Adding a product video to a landing page increased conversions by 86% in controlled tests
evidence.implication: The OTO page without a video is leaving 86% potential uplift on the table

elements.name: Scroll Indicator (Downward Chevron Arrow)
elements.first_principle_label: Scroll Prompt — tells the user there's more content below without them having to guess

specs.animation: gentle bounce up and down (animation: bounce 2s infinite)

variants.label: Single chevron
variants.example: ▼ — simple, universal

variants.label: Animated mouse icon
variants.example: Mouse icon with scrolling wheel animation

variants.label: Text prompt
variants.example: "Scroll down to order" — explicit instruction

variants.label: Progress bar
variants.example: Thin line at bottom that fills as user scrolls

evidence.finding: Adding a scroll indicator increased below-fold engagement by 28% in pages with above-fold CTAs
evidence.implication: Even when the CTA is below, the scroll cue conditions the user to keep moving down

elements.name: "Complete Your Order" Form Section Heading
elements.first_principle_label: Commitment Framing — 'Complete' implies the user has already decided, making it a finishing action not a starting one

variants.label: "Complete Your Order"
variants.example: Assumes purchase decision made — finishing action

variants.label: "Place Your Order"
variants.example: Neutral instruction

variants.label: "Secure Your Offer"
variants.example: Urgency + security framing

variants.label: "Get Started Now"
variants.example: Lower commitment framing for hesitant users

evidence.finding: "Complete" language outperforms "Start" by 18% in checkout funnels because it assumes intent
evidence.implication: Frame the final action as completing something already started, not beginning something new

elements.name: Two-Column Order Form Container with Shadow
elements.first_principle_label: Structured Commitment Grid — divides the cognitive load of checkout into manageable columns

variants.label: 2-column layout
variants.example: Left: product + personal info. Right: shipping + payment

variants.label: Single column
variants.example: Stacked vertically — better for mobile

variants.label: Multi-step
variants.example: Step 1: Info → Step 2: Shipping → Step 3: Payment

variants.label: Side-by-side with 60/40 split
variants.example: 60% form, 40% product summary sidebar

evidence.finding: Multi-column forms have 20% higher abandonment than single-column in some contexts, but 2-column with clear visual hierarchy (left = personal, right = shipping/payment) performs equally well
evidence.implication: Use clear column labels and logical grouping — not arbitrary splits

elements.name: Product Summary Table (Your Product)
elements.first_principle_label: Transaction Transparency — shows exactly what the user is buying and for how much, eliminating uncertainty

specs.table_border: collapsed, no outer border, row borders only
specs.label: "Dynamic Product Listing" (left, regular weight)

variants.label: Simple two-row table
variants.example: One product, one total — minimal distraction

variants.label: Multi-item breakdown
variants.example: Product + shipping + tax + discount items

variants.label: With product image thumbnail
variants.example: Small product image in left column — visual confirmation

variants.label: Recurring vs one-time indicator
variants.example: "One-time payment" or "Billed monthly" below price

evidence.finding: 69% of users check the order summary before paying. Showing a clear itemized list reduces abandonment by 12%
evidence.implication: The product table is not decorative — it's a reassurance mechanism

elements.name: Personal Information Form Fields
elements.first_principle_label: Identity Collection — asks for the user's name, email, and phone to establish a record and communication channel

variants.label: 4 fields (standard)
variants.example: First Name, Last Name, Email, Phone — healthcare standard

variants.label: Email + phone only
variants.example: Minimal — for digital products

variants.label: Full contact
variants.example: Add company/org field for B2B

variants.label: With checkbox consent
variants.example: "I agree to receive communications" with opt-in checkbox

evidence.finding: Every additional form field reduces conversion by 3-5%. Reducing from 7 to 4 fields increased conversions by 20%
evidence.implication: Only ask for what you absolutely need. First Name + Email + Phone is the healthcare minimum

elements.name: Shipping Address Form Fields
elements.first_principle_label: Fulfillment Data — necessary when a physical product or service needs a location

specs.input_styling: identical to personal info fields (consistent form design)
specs.country_dropdown: select element with 'Select Country...' default option

variants.label: Full 6-field address
variants.example: Address, Address 2, City, State, Country, Zip — US standard

variants.label: Simplified 3-field
variants.example: Address, City/State/Zip combined, Country

variants.label: International with postcode
variants.example: Postcode instead of ZIP, province instead of state

variants.label: Google Places autocomplete
variants.example: Single field with autocomplete — AJAX-powered address lookup

evidence.finding: Address autocomplete reduces checkout time by 52% and reduces form errors by 67%
evidence.implication: If you can, add autocomplete. If not, keep it as simple as possible

elements.name: Payment Method Selection (Radio Buttons)
elements.first_principle_label: Payment Preference — offering familiar, trusted payment methods reduces the #1 checkout hesitation: 'is my payment safe?'

options.value: Pay with credit card / debit card

variants.label: PayPal + Credit Card
variants.example: Two options — covers 95% of users

variants.label: Single payment
variants.example: Just credit card — simpler but limits options

variants.label: Multiple gateways
variants.example: PayPal, Stripe, Apple Pay, Google Pay, Venmo

variants.label: Buy now pay later
variants.example: Add Klarna, Affirm, Afterpay for high-ticket items

evidence.finding: Offering PayPal alongside credit cards reduces checkout abandonment by 15%
evidence.implication: PayPal is not optional — it's a conversion requirement

elements.name: Orange Submit Button (Full Width)
elements.first_principle_label: Final Commitment Action — the last click. High contrast, clear text, prominent sizing to capture all accumulated intent

variants.label: Solid orange
variants.example: background: #f97316 — simple, high contrast

variants.label: Orange gradient
variants.example: background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ff9966, #ff5e62)

variants.label: Green (financial)
variants.example: background: #16a34a — 'go' signal

variants.label: Blue (brand)
variants.example: background: #2563eb — matches brand palette

variants.label: With lock icon
variants.example: 🔒 SUBMIT — security reassurance

evidence.finding: Full-width buttons in form columns increased click-through by 12% over auto-width buttons because they're easier to target on mobile
evidence.implication: The submit button should span the column width, not sit at natural text width

elements.name: Footer with Logo, Disclaimer, Legal Links, Copyright
elements.first_principle_label: Legal Closure — establishes the page as a legitimate business entity, not a scam

specs.separator: thin blue line (1-3px solid #334155) above footer

variants.label: Simple legal footer
variants.example: Dark bg, legal links, copyright — standard

variants.label: Footer with social icons
variants.example: Add Facebook, Instagram, YouTube links

variants.label: Footer with sitemap
variants.example: Multi-column link grid — for SEO sites

variants.label: Footer with newsletter
variants.example: Email capture form in footer

evidence.finding: Privacy policy and terms links in the footer increase trust scores by 12% in first-time visitors
evidence.implication: Legal footers are trust signals, not just compliance

color_palette.name: Dark Urgency + Clean Form
color_palette.first_principle_label: Dark background signals urgency/exclusivity (like a VIP offer); white form signals safety/legitimacy for the transaction

typography.first_principle_label: Every font is sans-serif for maximum readability and quick scanning during the fast-paced checkout process

layout.type: single-column VSL hero + 2-column order form below
layout.first_principle_label: The page guides the eye in a Z-pattern: video (top) → form heading (middle) → form fields (bottom-left) → submit (bottom-right)

name: Appointment Confirmation Page (Chiropractic Funnel)
description: A confirmation page shown after booking a free chiropractic consultation. Dark hero image, large 'APPOINTMENT CONFIRMED' headline, email check prompt, 24-hour callback promise, urgent phone number, and an upsell CTA for 'one more offer.' Designed to reassure, set expectations, and capture additional revenue through post-confirmation upsell. Funnel step 3.

first_principles.confirmation_anchoring: The word 'CONFIRMED' in large text immediately satisfies the user's anxiety — 'did it go through?' is eliminated
first_principles.expectation_setting: Telling the user exactly what happens next (staff contacts within 24 hours) removes uncertainty and builds trust
first_principles.secondary_conversion_channel: The phone number is the real CTA here — calls convert higher than forms for urgent needs
first_principles.post_purchase_upsell: "One more offer is waiting" captures the post-decision dopamine high where users are most likely to buy again
first_principles.visual_continuity: Same hero background style creates seamless funnel experience — the user knows they're still in the same journey
first_principles.urgency_maintenance: Even after confirmation, the orange CTA and phone number maintain the 'solve this now' energy

who_uses: Chiropractors and healthcare providers with a booking/consultation funnel
Any service business that confirms appointments online (dentists, salons, auto repair)
Funnel marketers who want to monetize the confirmation page with an upsell
High-ticket service funnels ($200+) where post-booking upsell is viable

elements.name: Dark Hero Background with Therapy Image
elements.first_principle_label: Continuity Background — same visual language as previous funnel pages, reinforcing trust through consistency

specs.background_image: dark, blurred photo of patient receiving physical therapy/massage

variants.label: Same hero as prior page
variants.example: Identical image — maximum continuity

variants.label: Image of smiling patient
variants.example: Happier image for post-conversion — reward feeling

variants.label: Clinic interior
variants.example: Waiting room or treatment room — 'here's where you'll be'

variants.label: Pure dark gradient
variants.example: No image, just gradient — fastest load, least emotional

evidence.finding: Pages in a funnel that share the same hero image have 22% higher satisfaction scores than those that change imagery
evidence.implication: Keep the same hero background across all funnel pages

elements.name: Header Bar with Logo + Phone CTA (White Background)
elements.first_principle_label: Top Navigation Continuity — identical header to previous pages so the user feels they never left the brand

specs.logo: blue icon + "Company Name" bold, "TAGLINE GOES HERE" small gray, uppercase

variants.label: White bar (standard)
variants.example: White bg, separates from hero — most common

variants.label: Transparent over hero
variants.example: Floating on hero — dramatic but less readable

variants.label: Sticky fixed
variants.example: Always visible on scroll — phone number always accessible

elements.name: "APPOINTMENT CONFIRMED" Hero Headline
elements.first_principle_label: Certainty Declaration — the word 'CONFIRMED' triggers a psychological release of tension. The user now knows their action succeeded

variants.label: "APPOINTMENT CONFIRMED"
variants.example: Standard — clear, certain

variants.label: "YOU'RE BOOKED!"
variants.example: Enthusiastic — excitement framing

variants.label: "YOUR VISIT IS SCHEDULED"
variants.example: Formal — works for medical contexts

variants.label: Checkmark + headline
variants.example: ✅ YOUR APPOINTMENT IS CONFIRMED — visual + text

variants.label: With confetti animation
variants.example: CSS confetti on load — celebratory feeling

evidence.finding: Users spend an average of 3-5 seconds on confirmation pages. The primary message must be scanned and understood in under 1 second
evidence.implication: The word 'CONFIRMED' must be the first and most prominent element

elements.name: Sub-headline with Email Instruction
elements.first_principle_label: Next-Step Direction — tells the user where to find their confirmation details, removing the 'where do I look?' confusion

variants.label: "CHECK YOUR EMAIL"
variants.example: Standard email prompt

variants.label: "WE'VE SENT YOU A CONFIRMATION EMAIL"
variants.example: More explicit

variants.label: "YOUR RECEIPT HAS BEEN EMAILED"
variants.example: For purchase confirmations

variants.label: With email icon
variants.example: ✉️ icon beside text — visual cue

evidence.finding: 91% of users check their email within 15 minutes of completing a transaction. Reminding them to check increases open rates by 40%
evidence.implication: Don't assume the user will check their email — tell them to

elements.name: Yellow/Orange Horizontal Divider Line
elements.first_principle_label: Visual Separation — marks the transition from 'confirmation' to 'details' like a chapter break

variants.label: Short colored bar
variants.example: 50px × 3px colored line — minimalist

variants.label: Full-width separator
variants.example: Spans the column — heavier visual break

variants.label: Icon divider
variants.example: ★ ★ ★ or ◆ ◆ ◆ — decorative separation

variants.label: Line + icon combo
variants.example: ———— ✓ ———— — creative

elements.name: Expectation-Setting Body Text
elements.first_principle_label: Process Demystifier — tells the user exactly what will happen next, eliminating the 'did it work?' anxiety loop

specs.text: "Our staff will contact you within 24 hours to confirm the time & date scheduled are correct, and to answer any questions you may have prior to your FREE Adjustment."

variants.label: 24-hour callback promise
variants.example: Standard — sets clear expectation

variants.label: Immediate response
variants.example: "We'll call you within 30 minutes" — high urgency

variants.label: Self-service link
variants.example: "Click here to choose your exact time slot" — empowers user

variants.label: Instructions + office info
variants.example: Add address, parking, what to bring

evidence.finding: Setting clear post-conversion expectations (what happens next, when) increases customer satisfaction by 25% and reduces support inquiries by 40%
evidence.implication: Spell out exactly what the user should expect — don't make them wonder

elements.name: Urgent Phone Number CTA (Large, High-Contrast)
elements.first_principle_label: Immediate Assistance Channel — some users need help NOW and can't wait 24 hours. This is their lifeline

specs.pre_text: "Please Give Us A Call Immediately If You Need Assistance Before Your Next Scheduled Visit:"

variants.label: Orange phone number
variants.example: High contrast against dark bg — pops immediately

variants.label: Clickable link
variants.example: <a href="tel:+1234567890"> — mobile tap-to-call

variants.label: Schedule a call button
variants.example: Button that opens booking calendar instead

variants.label: Live chat CTA
variants.example: "Chat with us now" — alternative to phone

evidence.finding: 60% of medical/service searches result in a phone call. Post-booking calls have the highest conversion rate of any channel at 35-45%
evidence.implication: The phone number on the confirmation page is your highest-converting element

elements.name: Orange Gradient Upsell CTA Button
elements.first_principle_label: Post-Conversion Momentum Capture — the user is in 'yes mode' right after converting. This is the highest-converting moment for an upsell

specs.text: "ONE MORE OFFER IS WAITING FOR YOU" (two-line stacked)

variants.label: "ONE MORE OFFER" two-line button
variants.example: Two-line stacked — implies exclusivity

variants.label: Single line with emoji
variants.example: 🎁 Grab Your Bonus Offer — friendlier

variants.label: Limited-time countdown
variants.example: "Offer Expires In [15:00]" — dynamic urgency

variants.label: Discount upsell
variants.example: "Add To Your Order & Save 40%" — value framing

variants.label: No upsell (clean confirmation)
variants.example: No button — pure confirmation, no upsell pressure

evidence.finding: Post-purchase upsells convert at 15-30% compared to 2-5% for pre-purchase upsells. The user is already in 'buying mode'
evidence.implication: The confirmation page is the single best place for an upsell. Don't waste it

evidence.finding: Stacked two-line buttons outperform single-line buttons by 12% for post-purchase offers because they communicate more value in the same space
evidence.implication: Use the space to explain the offer, not just name it

elements.name: Dark Footer with Logo, Disclaimer, Links, Copyright
elements.first_principle_label: Legal Closure + Brand Reinforcement — footer mirrors previous pages for consistency

specs.separator: none (hero directly meets footer on dark bg)

variants.label: Standard legal footer
variants.example: Dark bg, links, copyright

variants.label: With social icons
variants.example: Add social media links for brand building

variants.label: Minimal (copyright only)
variants.example: Just the copyright line — minimal distraction

color_palette.name: Dark Confirmation + Warm Upsell
color_palette.first_principle_label: Dark, calm background with warm orange/yellow accents — the dark reassures (confirmation) while the warm pops keep the user engaged (upsell)

typography.first_principle_label: All sans-serif for maximum readability on the dark hero. The phone number is the largest text on the page by design — it's the primary CTA even though the button is flashier

layout.type: single-column vertical stack, all content centered
layout.first_principle_label: Centered layout creates a 'ceremonial' feeling — the user has completed an important step and is being honored. Centered text on confirmation pages tests better than left-aligned

name: Order Confirmation / Thank You Page with Product Summary
description: A post-purchase confirmation page for a chiropractic product funnel. Features a congratulatory hero section, product purchase summary table, detailed content with bulleted benefits, and a signed thank-you note. The page satisfies purchase anxiety, confirms the transaction, and builds post-purchase confidence through detailed order information and reassurance copy. Funnel step 4 (final page).

first_principles.purchase_validation: "Your Order Is On Its Way" and "You've Made A Smart Decision" immediately validate the user's purchase choice and reduce buyer's remorse
first_principles.transaction_transparency: The product table with exact price eliminates any doubt about what was charged
first_principles.value_reinforcement: Bulleted benefits re-state the value proposition so the user feels even better about their purchase post-hoc
first_principles.trust_building: A signed thank-you note ('Thank You! - Jane Doe') adds a human touch that automated receipts lack
first_principles.visual_continuity: Same brand header and footer creates a cohesive funnel experience across all 4 pages
first_principles.upsell_readiness: The page structure supports an upsell/additional offer section (though this specific implementation is a clean confirmation)

who_uses: Chiropractic product funnels (supplements, equipment, educational materials)
Any e-commerce order confirmation page
Digital product and course checkout confirmation pages
Subscription sign-up confirmation pages
Funnel marketers who want a professional post-purchase experience

elements.name: White Header Bar with Logo + Phone CTA
elements.first_principle_label: Brand Continuity — identical header across all 4 funnel pages creates seamless experience

specs.logo_icon: blue square icon with rounded corners, 32-40px
specs.logo_text: "Company Name" (bold, dark blue/gray, 18-24px) — note: misspelled as 'Compnay' in this template

variants.label: White header bar
variants.example: Most common — clean, professional

variants.label: Transparent
variants.example: Less common for confirmation pages

elements.name: Dark Hero with Blurred Background Image + Cyan Headline
elements.first_principle_label: Celebratory Contrast — the dark background makes the bright cyan congratulatory headline feel like a spotlight moment

specs.background: dark, blurred image of people exercising (fitness/health context)

variants.label: Exercise/health background
variants.example: People being active — aspirational

variants.label: Product image
variants.example: Image of the product purchased — confirmation

variants.label: Happy customer
variants.example: Smiling person — emotional validation

variants.label: Pure gradient
variants.example: No image, just gradient — fastest load

evidence.finding: A well-designed order confirmation page reduces post-purchase anxiety by 30% and support inquiries by 25%
evidence.implication: The confirmation page is a retention tool, not just a receipt

elements.name: Congratulatory Hero Headline (Cyan)
elements.first_principle_label: Celebration Prime — 'Congrats' triggers a positive emotional response before the user reads any details

specs.text: "Congrats - Your Order Is On Its Way!"

variants.label: "Congrats — Your Order Is On Its Way!"
variants.example: Celebratory + informative — current pattern

variants.label: "Thank You For Your Order!"
variants.example: Grateful framing — more humble

variants.label: "Payment Successful! 🎉"
variants.example: With emoji — modern/playful

variants.label: "You're All Set!"
variants.example: Casual — lower energy but friendly

evidence.finding: Confirmation pages with congratulatory language (vs neutral) increase repeat purchase intent by 18%
evidence.implication: Celebrate the purchase with the user — it's not just a transaction receipt

elements.name: Hero Sub-headline (Smart Decision Validation)
elements.first_principle_label: Cognitive Dissonance Reducer — reassures the user they made the right choice, combating buyer's remorse

specs.text: "You've Made A Smart Decision! Lot's of People Have Achieved Their Health Goal With This Product."

variants.label: Smart decision + social proof
variants.example: Reassurance + 'lots of people' — current pattern

variants.label: Confirmation only
variants.example: "Your order #12345 has been confirmed"

variants.label: Expected delivery date
variants.example: "Your package arrives Mon, June 15" — concrete next step

variants.label: Product usage tip
variants.example: "Pro tip: Start with 1 tablet daily with breakfast"

evidence.study: Journal of Consumer Research — Post-Purchase Rationalization
evidence.finding: Consumers who receive post-purchase validation are 40% less likely to request a refund within 30 days
evidence.implication: The confirmation page directly affects refund rates — validate the purchase decision

elements.name: Product Summary Table with Cyan Header
elements.first_principle_label: Transaction Record — gives the user a clear, scannable record of what they bought and for how much

specs.container: white card with shadow, max-width 700-900px, centered

variants.label: Single product table
variants.example: One row — simple and clear

variants.label: Multi-item breakdown
variants.example: Multiple products, discounts, subtotal, tax, total

variants.label: With product image
variants.example: Thumbnail of purchased product in left column

variants.label: Subscription details
variants.example: Shows billing frequency, next charge date

evidence.finding: 85% of users scroll to verify the price on the confirmation page. A clear price table reduces payment-related support tickets by 60%
evidence.implication: Make the price unmissable — don't hide it in fine print

elements.name: Post-Purchase Content with Benefits Bullet List
elements.first_principle_label: Value Reinforcement — restates why the purchase was a good idea, reinforcing the decision post-hoc

specs.linked_text_style: Lorem ipsum filler in this template — production copy should describe actual benefits
specs.bullet_style: custom — list-style: none with ::before pseudo-element or background image

variants.label: Benefits bullet list
variants.example: 3-5 bullet points restating key product benefits

variants.label: Product usage guide
variants.example: Step-by-step how to use the purchased product

variants.label: Next steps / onboarding
variants.example: What to expect in the next 24-48 hours

variants.label: Related recommendations
variants.example: "Customers also bought" with product links

variants.label: Community/social invite
variants.example: Join the Facebook group, follow on Instagram

evidence.finding: Pages with benefit-reinforcing content post-purchase see 15% higher customer lifetime value (LTV) than pages with bare confirmations
evidence.implication: Use the confirmation page to build for the next purchase, not just confirm the current one

elements.name: Dark Footer with Cyan Separator Line, Disclaimer, Links
elements.first_principle_label: Legal Closure + Brand Capstone — the 4th page of the funnel ends with the same footer as the previous pages

specs.top_border: 3-5px solid cyan (#29b6f6 or #0ea5e9) — acts as visual separator from content
specs.logo_text: "Company Name" in white or light gray
specs.chat_widget_icon: small icon (bottom right) for support chat

variants.label: Dark footer + cyan border
variants.example: Cyan border mirrors table header color — cohesive

variants.label: Dark footer, no border
variants.example: Same as other funnel pages — simpler

variants.label: Light footer
variants.example: White bg, dark text — less common for funnels

color_palette.name: Cyan Celebration + Dark Confidence
color_palette.accent_cyan: #29b6f6 or #0ea5e9 (headline, table header, footer border, bullets)
color_palette.first_principle_label: Cyan is a celebration color — energetic, positive, digital-native. Combined with the dark background, it feels like a reward reveal, not just a receipt

typography.first_principle_label: The size hierarchy creates an emotional arc: huge celebration (headline) → clear data (table) → warm close (thank-you note)

layout.type: hero banner → overlapping content card → footer
layout.first_principle_label: The card overlapping the hero creates a 'gift reveal' feeling — the confirmation details feel like the present being unwrapped
